question: Explain the concept of backward induction and describe the general steps used to find a sub-game perfect equilibrium in a finite horizon game of perfect information.
expectedPoints: Backward induction is a solution method that starts from the final nodes of the game tree and works backwards to the root.
At each decision node, the algorithm determines the optimal action for the player whose turn it is, assuming subsequent play will be optimal.
The process results in a strategy profile that specifies an optimal action at every sub-game, fulfilling the requirement of a sub-game perfect equilibrium.

question: Describe a key limitation of using backward induction to find sub-game perfect equilibria and explain why a Nash equilibrium might not be sub-game perfect.
expectedPoints: A key limitation is that it relies heavily on the assumption of common knowledge of rationality, where all players are rational and this is universally known.
A Nash equilibrium can include non-credible threats or promises, which are strategies that are optimal only if a player believes an irrational action will be taken later.
A sub-game perfect equilibrium refines Nash by eliminating these non-credible plans, requiring the strategy to be a Nash equilibrium in every sub-game.

question: Identify the primary characteristic that distinguishes a sub-game perfect equilibrium from a standard Nash equilibrium.
expectedPoints: A sub-game perfect equilibrium is a strategy profile that constitutes a Nash equilibrium in every sub-game of the original game.
